verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Now I ask again, whence have my accusers obtained this letter also?

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: I would learn of them who it was that put it into their hands?

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Do you cause them to answer.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: By this you may perceive that they have forged this, as they spread abroad also the former letter, which they published against me, with reference to the ill-named Magnentius.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: And being convicted in this instance also, on what pretence next will they bring me to make my defence?

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Their only concern is, to throw everything into disorder and confusion; and for this end I perceive they exercise their zeal.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: Perhaps they think that by frequent repetition of their charges, they will at last exasperate you against me.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: But you ought to turn away from such persons, and to hate them; for such as themselves are, such also they imagine those to be who listen to them; and they think that their calumnies will prevail even before you.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: The accusation of Doeg prevailed of old against the priests of God: but it was the unrighteous Saul, who hearkened unto him.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: And Jezebel was able to injure the most religious Naboth by her false accusations; but then it was the wicked and apostate Ahab who hearkened unto her.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: But the most holy David, whose example it becomes you to follow, as all pray that you may, favours not such men, but was wont to turn away from them and avoid them, as raging dogs.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: He says, ‘Whoso privily slandereth his neighbour, him will I destroy.’

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: For he kept the commandment which says, ‘Thou shalt not receive a false report.’

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: And false are the reports of these men in your sight.

verses.book: Apology to the Emperor. (Apologia Ad Constantium.)
verses.text: You, like Solomon, have required of the Lord (and you ought to believe yourself to have obtained your desire), that it would seem good unto Him to remove far from you vain and lying words.
